courses.title: Introduction to Python Programming
courses.description: Learn Python from scratch — variables, control flow, functions, and more
courses.changelog: v2.0 — Added control flow unit, assessment quizzes, and all enrichment fields.
courses.instructor_notes: Most students struggle with variable scope — spend extra time on that topic. Demo type() interactively — students love seeing dynamic typing in action.

glossary.definition: A named location in memory that stores a value.
A reusable block of code that performs a specific task.
A control structure that repeats a block of code.
An ordered, mutable collection of elements in Python.
Program that executes Python code line by line.

categories.title: Python Fundamentals
categories.description: Core concepts every Python programmer needs

glossary.definition: An object whose value can be changed after creation.
An object whose value cannot be changed after creation.

case_studies.title: Automating Daily Tasks
case_studies.scenario: A company spends 2 hours daily on manual file organization. How could Python help?
questions: What Python modules would you use?
How would you structure the script?
What are the time savings if automated?

discussion_prompts: Why is Python considered a good first language?
What are the trade-offs of dynamic typing?

units.title: Variables and Data Types
units.description: Understand how Python stores and manages data
units.instructor_notes: Demo type() interactively. Students love seeing dynamic typing in action.

glossary.definition: An object whose value can be changed after creation.
An object whose value cannot be changed after creation.

performance_standards: 1.1 Variables are declared with appropriate names
1.2 Data types are correctly identified and used
discussion_prompts: How does Python's type system compare to Java or C++?

learning_outcomes.title: Understand Variables and Data Types
learning_outcomes.description: Learn how to declare and use variables in Python
discussion_prompts: Why do variable naming conventions matter in team projects?

subtopics.title: What is a Variable?
subtopics.content:
A variable is a named location in memory that stores a value. In Python, you don't need to declare the type explicitly — Python infers it from the value you assign.

Variables are created when you first assign a value to them.
key_points: Variable names must start with a letter or underscore

examples.title: Creating Variables
examples.explanation: Here's how you create variables in Python:
examples.code:
name = "John"
age = 25
height = 5.9
is_student = True

print(name)    # Output: John
print(age)     # Output: 25

discussion_prompts: What makes a good variable name?

subtopics.title: Python Data Types
subtopics.content:
Python has several built-in data types:

• str — Text strings: "Hello"
• int — Whole numbers: 42
• float — Decimal numbers: 3.14
• bool — True or False
• list — Ordered collection: [1, 2, 3]
• dict — Key-value pairs: {"name": "John"}
key_points: Use type() to check a variable's data type

examples.title: Checking Types
examples.code:
x = 10
y = "Hello"
z = 3.14

print(type(x))  # <class 'int'>
print(type(y))  # <class 'str'>
print(type(z))  # <class 'float'>

subtopics.title: Practice: Variables
subtopics.content:
Try these exercises:

1. Create variables for your name, age, and favorite color
2. Print a message using all three
3. Convert your age to a string and concatenate
4. Try converting an invalid string to int and observe the error

examples.title: Sample Solution
examples.explanation: Here's one way to solve the exercises:
examples.code:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
color = "blue"

print(f"Hi, I'm {name}, I'm {age} years old, and I like {color}")
print("Age as string: " + str(age))

subtopics.title: Quick Quiz: Variables
subtopics.content: Test your understanding of variables and data types.

quiz.question: What is the output of: x = 5; print(type(x))?
quiz.explanation: 5 is an integer, so type(x) returns <class 'int'>

quiz.question: Which is a valid Python variable name?
quiz.explanation: _student is valid. 2nd_place starts with a number, my-name has a hyphen, and 'class' is a reserved keyword.

quiz.question: What type is: x = 3.14?
quiz.explanation: 3.14 has a decimal point, making it a float.

learning_outcomes.title: Input and Output Operations
learning_outcomes.description: Learn to read user input and display formatted output

subtopics.title: The print() Function
subtopics.content:
The print() function outputs text to the console. You can print strings, numbers, variables, and combine them using f-strings.

f-strings (formatted string literals) let you embed expressions inside string literals.
key_points: f-strings use f"..." syntax with {variable} placeholders
The sep and end parameters control formatting

examples.title: f-strings in Action
examples.code:
name = "World"
print(f"Hello, {name}!")

age = 25
print(f"In 10 years, you'll be {age + 10}")

subtopics.title: The input() Function
subtopics.content: The input() function reads a line of text from the user. It always returns a string, so you need to convert it for numbers.
key_points: Use int() or float() to convert for math

examples.title: Getting User Input
examples.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print(f"Hi {name}, you are {age} years old.")

subtopics.title: Quick Quiz: I/O
subtopics.content: Test your understanding of input and output.

quiz.question: What does input() always return?
quiz.explanation: input() always returns a string, even if the user types a number.

quiz.question: Which converts a string to an integer?
quiz.explanation: int() converts a string to an integer.

units.title: Control Flow
units.description: Learn if/else statements, loops, and program flow control
units.instructor_notes: Start with real-world examples of decision-making before introducing syntax.

glossary.definition: A statement that executes different code based on a boolean expression.
Repeating a block of code using a loop.
A statement that exits a loop immediately.

performance_standards: 2.2 for and while loops are appropriately chosen
2.3 Loop control (break, continue) is used properly

case_studies.title: Building a Login System
case_studies.scenario: A website needs a login system with 3 attempts before locking out.
questions: How would you track failed attempts?
How do you handle the lockout?

discussion_prompts: When should you use a for loop vs a while loop?
What are the dangers of infinite loops?

learning_outcomes.title: Write Conditional Statements
learning_outcomes.description: Use if, elif, and else to control program flow

subtopics.title: If, Elif, Else
subtopics.content:
Conditional statements let your program make decisions:

• if — runs code if a condition is True
• elif — checks another condition if the first is False
• else — runs if no conditions matched
key_points: Conditions evaluate to True or False
You can chain multiple elif statements
else catches everything not matched above

examples.title: Grade Calculator
examples.explanation: Determine a letter grade from a score:

subtopics.title: Quick Quiz: Conditionals
subtopics.content: Test your understanding of conditional statements.

quiz.explanation: x > 5 is True, so it prints 'A'. The elif is never checked because the first condition matched.

quiz.question: Which operator checks if two values are equal?

Cascading Style Sheets — styling and layout of web pages.

units.instructor_notes: Show a rendered page alongside its source code.

learning_outcomes.title: Identify Common HTML Tags
learning_outcomes.description: Recognize and use common HTML elements

subtopics.title: Common HTML Elements
subtopics.content:
HTML uses tags to structure content: •

to

— Headings •

— Paragraphs • — Links • — Images •

    /
      — Lists •
      — Block containers
key_points: Tags can nest inside each other

examples.title: Basic HTML Page
examples.code:
Hello World

This is my first page.

quiz.question: What tag creates the largest heading?
quiz.explanation: <h1> is the largest heading. <h6> is the smallest.

quiz.question: Which tag is self-closing?
quiz.explanation: is self-closing — it doesn't need a closing tag.
